title: A Practitioner’s Decision Tree for Panel-Data Causal Inference
text:
This site hosts an interactive decision tree that recommends a causal-inference estimator for panel data. The tree branches on the structure of the design — the number of ever-treated units, treatment timing, whether the treatment is absorbing, whether it is binary or continuous — and lands on a leaf with the primary recommended method, alternatives, identifying assumptions, caveats, the relevant R package, and a minimal code snippet.
The app was built as a heuristic for EUI students working on causal inference with panel data, in response to the dizzying pace of the recent literature on difference-in-differences-style estimators and their extensions. It is a work in progress — if you find a mistake, a missing method, or have a suggestion, please open an issue.

title: A Practitioner’s Decision Tree for Panel-Data Causal Inference
text:
The decision tree






TipFirst load is slow



The tree runs entirely in your browser via WebAssembly. The first load takes 10–20 seconds while R bootstraps; refreshing reuses the cache.




If the embedded version does not load, open the app in a new tab.

title: A Practitioner’s Decision Tree for Panel-Data Causal Inference
text:
How to use it

Answer one question at a time. Each question narrows the menu of estimators based on the structure of your data. You do not need to know the methods ahead of time.
Land on a leaf. Each leaf returns the primary method, alternatives, identifying assumptions, caveats, an R package, and minimal example code.
Use the breadcrumb at the top to backtrack one step, or the Start over button to reset.
Compare a few branches. If your data structure is borderline (e.g. almost absorbing, almost common timing), walk both branches and compare the recommendations. Divergence is informative.

title: A Practitioner’s Decision Tree for Panel-Data Causal Inference
text:
What the tree branches on







Question
Why it matters




How many units are ever treated?
One \(\Rightarrow\) synthetic-control family. A few \(\Rightarrow\) synthetic DiD. Ten or more \(\Rightarrow\) standard DiD machinery. All units treated \(\Rightarrow\) you need an extra source of variation (a baseline factor, an ineligible subgroup).


Binary or continuous treatment?
Continuous-dose designs require a different estimand (a dose-response curve, not a scalar) and different estimators.


Absorbing or non-absorbing?
Once-treated-always-treated unlocks Callaway–Sant’Anna, Sun–Abraham, Borusyak imputation, stacked DiD. Switchers / reversals require fect or DIDmultiplegt-style estimators.


Staggered or common timing?
Heterogeneity-robust estimators are required under staggered adoption; plain TWFE is unbiased only under common timing.


Conditional or unconditional parallel trends?
Doubly robust adjustments (Callaway–Sant’Anna with covariates, Sant’Anna–Zhao DR) when treated and control differ on observables that drive trends.



When the design admits no DiD-style answer (no untreated contrast and no extra leverage), the tree says so explicitly — and points you toward the relevant hybrid designs.

title: A Practitioner’s Decision Tree for Panel-Data Causal Inference
text:
Citing this tool
If you use the decision tree in teaching or applied work, please cite it as:

Valli, R. (2026). A Practitioner’s Decision Tree for Panel-Data Causal Inference [online application]. https://robertovalli.github.io/practitioner_guide_did/

title: A Practitioner’s Decision Tree for Panel-Data Causal Inference
text:
Acknowledgements
The tree was developed alongside the EUI workshop Advanced Counterfactual Methods for Causal Inference with Panel Data (Spring 2026). It draws on the recent econometrics literature, in particular the synthesis by Roth, Sant’Anna, Bilinski & Poe (2023), and on the implementation guidance shipped with the R packages it recommends.

title: A Practitioner’s Decision Tree for Panel-Data Causal Inference
